Isaiah's call to prophecy begins not with comfort but with overwhelming confrontation. The chapter opens with the death of King Uzziah—the human ruler upon whom Judah has relied—and immediately pivots to what matters infinitely more: the Lord seated on His throne, high and exalted. The seraphim cry "Holy, holy, holy," repeating the word three times in a way that echoes through eternity itself. This is not casual worship. The doorposts shake. Smoke fills the temple. Isaiah stands in the presence of absolute holiness, and his response is not wonder but terror. He cries out that he is ruined—completely undone—because he has unclean lips and lives among a people with unclean lips. He has glimpsed the King, the Lord of Hosts, and in that moment understands the depth of his own corruption. This is what genuine encounter with God does. It does not make us feel better about ourselves. It strips away pretense and exposes us for what we truly are.

Yet God does not leave Isaiah in that state of ruin. One of the seraphim takes a coal from the altar—the place of atonement—and touches Isaiah's lips with it. The prophet's iniquity is removed and his sin atoned for. This is not earned. It is given. Only after this cleansing does Isaiah hear the voice of God asking, "Whom shall I send?" The question itself is remarkable: God asks rather than commands. And Isaiah, now cleansed, responds with complete surrender: "Here am I. Send me!" This is what happens when a person truly encounters God's holiness and experiences His grace. Resistance breaks. Excuses vanish. The only reasonable response is to offer oneself entirely to His purposes.

But then the word God gives Isaiah to speak is devastating. He is to speak in such a way that people will hear but not understand, see but not perceive. He is to make their hearts calloused, deafen their ears, and close their eyes—otherwise they might turn and be healed. This is not because God delights in their destruction. Rather, this is judgment that matches the hardness of their refusal. God has made His message clear; Isaiah will make it clearer still. And yet the people will not listen. They will reject what they hear. Their judgment will be complete—cities ruined, houses empty, the land desolate. Yet even here, at the chapter's end, there is a strange word: a tenth remains, like a stump left when a great tree is felled. That stump is holy seed. God will preserve a remnant. Destruction will not be final. This is the burden Isaiah accepts when he says, "Send me"—to speak truth that will be rejected, to warn a people who will not listen, to minister in a time of judgment, trusting that God's purposes include both ruin and redemption.

What strikes us most forcefully is that Isaiah does not know the full weight of his calling when he volunteers. He hears God's question and responds in faith, in the fresh reality of forgiveness. Then God tells him what his message will accomplish—and it is bitter. Yet Isaiah has already said yes. He is bound now to faithfulness regardless of response, regardless of whether his words bring healing or hardening. This is the nature of true obedience: it is not negotiated based on outcomes we can predict or control. It is the simple act of placing ourselves at God's disposal, trusting that His purposes will be accomplished through our weakness and our faithfulness, in ways we may never fully understand.
